issues.title: PromptTemplate validation prevents using maps in templates

comments.body: thanks for reporting this, will investigate after the M3 release.

Will be taking a look shortly.  Thanks for your patience and the suggestion.  Yes, perhaps validation is going a bit too far.  The idea was to fail fast, but perhaps the trade off here isn't worth it.

I'm running into the same issue and had to stop using `PromptTemplate` and going to low level StringTemplate `ST`, have it render the output string correctly and then pass it to the `Prompt`.

I appreciate the idea of failing fast and agree that the trade-offs probably aren't worth it.

Thanks for your patience.  

I was thinking to disable the validate the validation by default as a quick hack.

Not sure on what the mechanism would be to enable it.  Perhaps something along the lines of 'defaultPromptTemplateOptions(PromptTemplateOptions).  Could also work for changing the default delimiters. 

 I spent more time working on a design for this, it touches so much, but takes longer get right.  Doing what I suggested to change delimiters above would then render all the default text used in the advisors broken...

This has now been fixed, the PR is https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-ai/pull/2920 and searching for TemplateRenderer in the docs will show you how to use it. You can provide your own TemplateRender impls.

let us know how it goes. thanks for your patience.

You can specify a validation mode in the StTemplateRenderer impl, or make your own.  I suspect over time some other template engines will be submitted.
Great - thanks for the update. I'm using JTE at the moment for my prompts so may look at if I can get that to work with the TemplateRender interface.

issues.title: Native Memory Leak by EmbeddingModel.embed(someText)
issues.body: Hello, **Bug description** We discovered a memory leak after a few days we've put our application into production. Native memory usage is growing slowly until it crashes with an OutOfMemory error. **Environment** Linux or MacOSX Spring AI 1.0.0-SNAPSHOT as of 2024-09-27 Java 21 Using EmbeddingModel.embed() Model huggingface.co/intfloat/multilingual-e5-small **Steps to reproduce** ```java while (true) { embeddingModel.embed("some text to embed"); } ``` Observe process RAM usage grow slowly. It sometimes decreases a bit (as if some native code got some garbage collection done), but not as much as needed. **Expected behavior** Would be cool if it didn't crash in production :-) **Minimal Complete Reproducible example** Here is a real simple way to reproduce it: The application code: https://github.com/slaout/springai-embed-memoryleak-proof/blob/main/src/main/java/com/github/slaout/springai/embed/memoryleak/EmbedderMemoryLeakProof.java The application properties: https://github.com/slaout/springai-embed-memoryleak-proof/blob/main/src/main/resources/application.properties The Maven dependencies: https://github.com/slaout/springai-embed-memoryleak-proof/blob/main/pom.xml It embeds a random string as fast as possible (but it could also be the same string over and over). JVM RAM is normal and contained. But the java process RAM is leaking. This is the native memory part that is leaking. I think this is PyTorch native executable that is eating all that RAM. The application prints every seconds the RAM of the "java" Unix process. If we plot it over more than 2 hours (8000 seconds), RAM alywas increases until it doubles (blue graph). If we plot the delta from previous seconds (red graph), we see regular increases (and some seldom decreases, below 0) in a pattern. And in our Kubernetes environment, all pods crash at about the same time with an OutOfMemory error, restart (every one to two days for us), and our application does not respond because remaining pods are overflowed. The "top" command even reported memory growing from 2GB to 27GB during these two ours: Begin : ``` PID COMMAND %CPU TIME #TH #WQ #PORT MEM PURG CMPRS PGRP PPID STATE BOOSTS %CPU_ME %CPU_OTHRS 65832 java 830.3 01:48.61 73/9 1 206 2001M+ 0B 1546M- 48338 48338 running *0[1] 0.00000 0.00000 ``` End : ``` PID COMMAND %CPU TIME #TH #WQ #PORT MEM PURG CMPRS PGRP PPID STATE BOOSTS %CPU_ME %CPU_OTHRS 65832 java 759.5 19:33:43 72/10 1/1 198 27G+ 0B 24G+ 48338 48338 running *0[1] 0.00000 0.00000 ```` Is there anything we can do about it? Would it be Spring AI, DeepJavaLibrary (DJL), OnnxRuntime and/or PyTorch fault?

comments.body:
Looks like the inputs to ONNX Runtime aren't being closed, so it's leaking native memory allocated by ORT. I think `inputIds`, `tokenTypeIds` and `attentionMask` should be closed when the result object is closed in a finally at the end of the `session.run` call. 
https://github.com/spring-projects/spring-ai/blob/main/models/spring-ai-transformers/src/main/java/org/springframework/ai/transformers/TransformersEmbeddingModel.java#L260.
Thanks for debugging this @Craigacp , we will close those `OnnxTensor`s in a finally block

issues.title: Review Function Calling Epic for M3 timeframe
issues.body: review the function calling 'epic' in #1350  (where there is finer grained grouping of feature request for function calling features) and see what can be done for the M3 timeframe.
